coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric fluorescence and light scattering sensing platform based on Cu-doped carbon dots for tryptophan and Fe(III)
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Mu, Zhao
Hua, Jianhao
Feng, Shouai
Yang, Yaling
coredata.dc:description: A new Cu-doped carbon dots (Cu-CDs) were synthesized rapidly and simply via one-step thermolysis of Na2[Cu(EDTA)] and ascorbic acid (AA) at 250 °C for 2 h with a high quantum yield of 9.8%. The Cu-CDs exhibits two signals of fluorescence at 396 nm and second-order scattering (SOS) at 617 nm under a single excitation wavelength of 308 nm, and can be obviously enhanced by tryptophan (Trp) or Fe(III) leading to the ratiometric fluorescence and SOS response with a good linear wider range of 0.5–250 μM and 0.1–50 μM, respectively. This sensing system exhibits good selectivity and sensitivity toward Trp and Fe(III) over other analytes with a low detection limit of 275 nM and 46 nM, respectively. Furthermore, the proposed sensing system displays a prospective application for quantitative assay of Trp and Fe(III) in practical sample.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ratiometric sensing
Fluorescence and light scattering
Enhancement

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Possible fluorescence enhancement mechanism for Trp
Possible fluorescence enhancement mechanism for Fe(III)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram for the synthesis of Cu-CDs and ratiometric detection of tryptophan and Fe(III).

TEM image (A) (inset: HRTEM image), Size image (B), XRD pattern (C), FTIR spectra (D), XPS spectra (E) and high resolution XPS spectra of C 1s (F), O 1s (G), and Cu 2p (H) of the as-synthesized Cu-CDs.

UV–Vis absorption, SOS and fluorescence emission spectra (A) and Fluorescence excitation、emission and SOS spectra (B) of Cu-CDs. Fluorescence decay of the as-synthesized Cu-CDs (C). The effect of pH (D), different ionic strengths (E) and different storage time (F) on the intensity of fluorescence and SOS of Cu-CDs.

(A) The optimization of reaction time for detection tryptophan. (B) FL spectra of Cu-CDs after adding different concentrations of tryptophan (0–250 μM). (C) Linear relationship between Fluorescence/SOS and the concentration of tryptophan. (D) Fluorescence/SOS variation of Cu-CDs in the presence of glucose, AA and different amino acids. (E) UV–Vis absorption of Cu-CDs before and after adding tryptophan. (F) Effect of different target analytes on the fluorescence decay of Cu-CDs.

(A) The optimization of reaction time for detection Fe(III). (B) FL spectra of Cu-CDs after adding different concentrations of Fe(III) (0–50 μM). (C) Linear relationship between SOS/Fluorescence and the concentration of Fe(III). (D) SOS/Fluorescence variation of Cu-CDs in the presence of different metal ions. (E) UV–Vis absorption of Cu-CDs before and after adding Fe(III).
Determination of tryptophan in the milk samples. (n = 3).
Determination of Fe(III) in the lake water samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A ratiometric fluorescence and light scattering sensing platform based on Cu-doped carbon dots for tryptophan and Fe(III)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Yunnan Province 650500, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Life Science and Technology
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Technology Centre of China Tobacco Guangxi Industrial Co., LTD, 530001 Nanning, Guangxi Province, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Technology Centre of China Tobacco Guangxi Industrial Co., LTD

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new Cu-doped carbon dots (Cu-CDs) were synthesized rapidly and simply via one-step thermolysis of Na2[Cu(EDTA)] and ascorbic acid (AA) at 250 °C for 2 h with a high quantum yield of 9.8%. The Cu-CDs exhibits two signals of fluorescence at 396 nm and second-order scattering (SOS) at 617 nm under a single excitation wavelength of 308 nm, and can be obviously enhanced by tryptophan (Trp) or Fe(III) leading to the ratiometric fluorescence and SOS response with a good linear wider range of 0.5–250 μM and 0.1–50 μM, respectively. This sensing system exhibits good selectivity and sensitivity toward Trp and Fe(III) over other analytes with a low detection limit of 275 nM and 46 nM, respectively. Furthermore, the proposed sensing system displays a prospective application for quantitative assay of Trp and Fe(III) in practical sample.

ce:section.ce:para: Tryptophan (Trp) is an essential amino acid for human body and plays an important role in the construction of proteins and cell metabolism [1–3]. Trp deficiency has also been associated with Alzheimer's disease (AD), because increasing of Trp intake would increase the 5-HT neurotransmission and may be effective in decreasing pathological plaque in AD [4,5]. Moreover, manipulating Trp will influence of the mood, cognition and gut-brain axis, such alterations may be important in the pathophysiology of human central nervous system disorders [6]. So, Trp has to be taken by foods in daily diet to maintain the nitrogen balance. Trp is a usual constituent of most protein-based foods, then quantitative and sensitive detection of Trp in foods becoming important for the sake of public health. Furthermore, Fe(III) is a metal ion essential to both biological systems and environmental processes. Measurement in water of Fe(III) in living systems is necessary and meaningful because of the crucial roles of Fe(III) play in organisms fundamental metabolic processes [7], chemical [8], and environmental systems [9].

Owing to their selectivity, low toxicity, environmental friendliness, low cost and simple synthetic routes，carbon dots (CDs) exhibit great potential for fluorescence detection [10–17]. So far, some kinds of detection of tryptophan and Fe(III) using carbon dots have been reported [18–24]. However, the majority of these designs were just one fluorescence signal for sensing. A single fluorescence signal was easily influenced by the experimental conditions such as, instrumental condition, temperature, probe concentration, solvent polarity, etc. On this basis, established a method of dual signals sensor is fundamentally significant. The dual signals sensor provides built-in correction for environmental interference and eliminates the unstability of the source light intensity, so this sensor can increase the selectivity and sensitivity of the measurement. During the synthesis of the carbon dots, the original size of the carbon dots is reduced due to the doping of the metal elements, and the fluorescence quantum yield is improved. On the contrary, the formation of light scattering in the carbon dots is due to the aggregation-inducing effect. Thus, nanomaterials that adjust the physical and chemical properties associated with size and shape can simultaneously achieve fluorescence and light scattering responses [25]. On this basis, a new strategy for designing ratio sensor is proposed, which combines the two different independent signals of fluorescence and light scattering.

In the present work, we have synthesized a new Cu-doped dual signals (fluorescence and SOS (λem = 2λex)) probe by exploiting one-step thermolysis method. Shown in Fig. 1 , the procedure to fabricate the Cu-CDs was tremendously convenient, and no further modification was required. Different from the common synthesis of hybrid carbon dots through intrinsic interactions of the two components [26–31]. Moreover, this dual signals probe possesses unique optical properties that can be excited by single-wavelength, which could increase signal-to-noise ratio and thus much more reliable quantification. In the design process of the Cu-CDs detection system, electrons and unoccupied orbitals outside Cu2+ promote their chelation with the amino and carboxyl groups on the surface of the CDs. During the detection of tryptophan, Cu2+ promotes the chelation of Cu-CDs with amino and carboxyl groups on the surface of tryptophan. On the other hand, the doping of Cu2+ into CDs promotes the electron accepting and electron donating ability, and Fe(III) enters the surface pores of Cu-CDs and inhibits charge transfer. The detection of Fe(III) by the second-order scattering peak is realized [32–37]. Most importantly, this dual signals probe can selectivity response to Trp and Fe(III), and that fully realize the ratiometric sensing. The ratio of fluorescence/SOS (I396/I617) and SOS/fluorescence (I617/I396) can be enhance by addition of Trp and Fe(III) at low concentration. This newly proposed ratiometric probe is not only simple and inexpensive but also provides a new perspective for sensing applications of metal ions and amino acids.

Na2[Cu(EDTA)] were purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. The analytical reagent of ascorbic acid (AA) was obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. The ultrapure water used throughout all the experiments was purified through an EasyQ system (LAB-BIOGEN, Kunming, China). Inorganic salts Mg(NO3)2, BaCl2·2H2O，Cd(NO3)2，Hg (NO3)2·0.5H2O，KNO3, MnCl2, Cu(NO3)2, NaNO3, PbCl2, ZnCl2, FeCl3·6H2O, FeSO4·7H2O, glucose, AA, Trp, Leu, Glu, Cys, Cys-Cys, Asp, and Tyr were dissolved in ultrapure water(100 mL) to afford 1 mM aqueous solutions, respectively. All samples were prepared at room temperature.

The tube furnace (Shanghai sager, SG-GS1200, Shanghai, China) was used to synthesized of the Cu-CDs. The morphological evaluation was performed using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F30). The fluorescence spectra were obtained using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained on a UV–visible spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU, Japan). FT-IR spectrum of Cu-CDs was recorded by using a Tensor-27 FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). Another two Instruments of D8-advance X-ray diffractometer (XRD) (Bruker, Germany) and K-AlphaX photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. U.S.A.) were used to characterization the Cu-CDs.

The Cu-CDs were prepared by a one-step thermolysis method. A corundum boat filled with the mixture of 1.6 g Na2[Cu(EDTA)] and 0.1 g AA were placed in the center of a quartz tube, and calcined in a tube furnace under N2 atmosphere with an accelerating rate of 3 °C min−1 at 250 °C. After being calcined for 2 h, it was allowed to cool down naturally. The product was ground and dissolved in ultrapure water (100 mL), and the suspension was ultrasonically treated (300 W, 40 kHz) for 30 min at room temperature, and then centrifuged with 10,000 rpm for 20 min. The upper brown solution was separated out, filtered using a slow-speed quantitative millipore filter (0.25 μm) to remove solid residues. The obtained Cu-CDs were stored at 4 °C for future use.

The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of Cu-CDs was measured according to an established procedure by comparing the fluorescence intensities and absorption values of Cu-CDs samples with quinine sulfate. Quinine sulfate was dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 (literature quantum yield 0.54 at 360 nm) while Cu-CDs were dissolved in water. The QY of Cu-CDs was computed by the following equation:
Φs= Φr × (Is/Ir) × (Ar/As) × (ηs 2/ηr 2).

Where Φ is the fluorescence quantum yield (QY), I is the measured integrated emission intensity, A is the absorbance and η is the refractive index. The subscript s and r were behalf of sample and reference [38].

The ratiometric fluorescent detection of Trp and Fe(III) were performed at room temperature in water solution. For detection of Trp, in a typical experimental setup, 50 μL of the obtained Cu-CDs solution and different amounts of Trp were added into a 10.0 mL centrifuge tube, and the mixture was diluted to 4.0 mL with ultrapure water and vortex-mixed for 1 min to mix completely before spectral measurements. The fluorescence spectra of this ratiometric probe under a single wavelength excitation at 308 nm were recorded by a fluorescence spectrophotometer，the slit widths of excitation and emission were both 5 nm. In order to evaluate the selectivity of CDs for Trp and assess the interference of coexisting amino acids, the similar tests were performed for other amino acids: Leu, Glu, Cys, Cys-Cys, Asp and Tyr, including AA and glucose.

Meanwhile, for the detection of Fe(III), the detection method and steps were the similar as for Trp. The fluorescence spectra with added different amounts of Fe(III) under a single wavelength excitation at 308 nm were recorded by a fluorescence spectrophotometer. Simultaneously, the similar procedure was performed for the detection of other metal ions: Mg2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, K+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Na+, Pb2+，Fe2+, and Zn2+.

Lake water were chosen as real samples to evaluate the Cu-CDs as a probe for Fe(III). As described in the previous literature [39], the lake water, which obtained from the Dian Lake of Kunming, Yunnan province, China, were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min to remove solid impurities and then filtered through a 0.22 μm filtered membrane. The resulting solution was spiked with different amounts of Fe(III) and then analyzed with the standard addition method.

In order to assess the Cu-CDs as a probe for Trp in real samples, the milk was chosen as real samples. Bovine raw milk was purchased from a local farm of Kunming, Yunnan province, China. The specific treatment was as follows: firstly, bovine raw milk samples (50 mL) were heated to 40 °C in a water bath kettle. Secondly, 0.5 M HCl were added slowly into the samples under continuously stirring, then the pH of the solution were adjusted to 4.7. Thirdly, allowed the suspension cooled naturally to room temperature and then centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 5 min to remove precipitation. The resultant milk samples was spiked with Trp at different concentration levels and then analyzed with the same method as lake water.

Fig. 2A shows the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the synthesized Cu-CDs. The small particles are nearly spherical and the diameters of Cu-CDs mainly distribute in the narrow range of 2.2–4.6 nm with an average size of 3.48 nm (Fig. 2B). The HRTEM image shows the high crystallinity of the Cu-CDs with a lattice spacing of 0.22 nm. The XRD pattern in Fig. 2C shows a broad peak centered at 2θ = 29.1°, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphite. The small sizes of CDs results in a broad diffraction peak width. These results demonstrated that the one-step thermolysis method is efficient for the fabrication of Cu-CDs. The structure and surface functional groups of Cu-CDs were characterized with FTIR spectra. As shown in Fig. 2D, the broad absorption bands at 3343.02 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH. A peak at 2960.48 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibrations of CH.A peak at 1667.68 cm−1 is the result of the stretching vibrations of CO. The absorption bands from 1455.04 cm−1 to 1404.61 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibrations of CC and CN. A peak at 1297.97 cm−1 is originated from the stretching vibrations of CC. The absorption bands from 932.1 cm−1 to 1109.22 cm−1 are identified to the stretching vibrations of CO. The bending vibration of CH is corresponding to the absorption band at 678.54 cm−1. In addition, the stretching vibrations of CuO is located at 527.36 cm−1, further confirms the incorporation of copper during the synthesis of the Cu-CDs. These data indicate that Cu-CDs was mainly composed of C, O, N, and Cu. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy(XPS) used to determine the valence states of the elements in Cu-CDs. The full range XPS spectrum of Cu-CDs in Fig. 2E clearly shows five main peaks, for C 1s, N 1s, Na, O1s and Cu 2p, respectively. As shown in the C 1s XPS spectra in Fig. 2F, the carbon existed in three different states: CC/CC, CN, and CO/CO (284.5, 285.6, and 287.6 ev). The high resolution O 1s spectrum in Fig. 2G could be deconvoluted into three peaks of 530.5 ev (CO), 531.7 ev (COC), and 535.2 ev (COH), explaining teems with O-rich groups on the surface of carbon frame, further explains good water solubility of Cu-CDs. In addition, the Cu 2p spectrum (Fig. 2H) had three peaks: the peaks at 932.3 ev, 933.3 ev, and 952.4 ev assigned to Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 spectra, illustrating the presence of Cu2+ and Cu0 in Cu-CDs.

The optical properties of the Cu-CDs were also examined [40]. The UV–vis spectrum of the Cu-CDs is shown in Fig. 3A. A typical adsorption peak at ~270 nm is observed and assigned to the π–π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains, which dominate the Cu-CDs structure. The peak at ~325 nm, a broad UV–vis absorption is attributed to the n–π* transition of CO. The luminescence of the carbon dots can be generated by the sp2 hybrid structure, and the band gap of the carbon dots can be regulated by changing the sp2 content, thereby realizing the regulation of the fluorescence emission behavior of the carbon dots [41–43]. As it can be seen in Fig. 3B, the FL spectra shows that the two maximum signals peaks of Cu-CDs at 396 nm and 617 nm as it is excited at 308 nm. Obviously, the fluorescence signal is displayed at 396 nm and the second-order scattering signal is displayed at 617 nm. Excitation-dependent PL behavior is typical and common in carbon fluorescent materials. The narrow FL spectra shape signified that the as-obtained particles of Cu-CDs possess character of monodisperse particle. In addition, the solution of Cu-CDs emits bright blue luminescence under the irradiation of UV- light (365 nm). The quantum yield (QY) of Cu-CDs in water was measured to be 9.8% using quinine sulfate as a reference. The fluorescence lifetime of Cu-CDs could be measured by time-resolved fluorescence. The laser light source collects fluorescent photons under the conditions of an excitation wavelength of 360 nm and an emission wavelength of 410 nm. As show in Fig. 3C, the fluorescence lifetime of the CDs was 13.22 ns. This short lifetime indicated that the PL mechanism may be due to the radioactive recombination of excitations [44]. Moreover, as the image of Fig. 3D shown, the fluorescence and SOS intensity of Cu-CDs in various solutions with different pH levels from 2.2 to 8.0 had no significant variation. The influence of different ionic strengths on the fluorescence and SOS intensity of Cu-CDs was also evaluated in NaCl solution with various concentrations from 0 to 1.0 M. There are nearly no changes in fluorescence and SOS intensity as shown in Fig. 3E. Moreover, Fig. 3F shows the effect of storage time on the intensity of fluorescence and SOS of Cu-CDs. It can be seen that the intensity of Cu-CDs does not change significantly with the prolongation of storage time, indicating that Cu-CDs have strong resistant to photobleaching ability. These results indicated that the as-synthesized Cu-CDs had the excellent optical properties with stability, which can be used as a highly sensitive sensor.

In this study, an interesting ratiometric probe was be synthesized, which can enhance the fluorescence intensity with addition of Trp into the present Cu-CDs aqueous solution. To verify the response rate of the fluorescence signal of Cu-CDs to Trp, 50 μL of the obtained Cu-CDs solution and 1 mL of Trp (1 mM) were added into a 10.0 mL centrifuge tube, and the mixture was diluted to 4.0 mL with ultrapure water and vortex-mixed for 1 min and then incubated for 0–60 min. Fig. 4A shows the relationship between the time with the ratio of fluorescence and SOS intensity, easy to see that the ratio almost have no changed after 10 min. Therefore, 10 min was the most suitable reaction time in the following studies to determination for Trp. Under optimized conditions, Fig. 4B shows the fluorescence emission and SOS spectra with 308 nm excitation, with increasing concentration of Trp added, the fluorescence intensity of Cu-CDs at 396 nm could be enhance, while the SOS intensity at 617 nm was kept constant. So fluorescence/SOS can be used to expression the change of fluorescence intensity with addition for different concentration of Trp. Thus, the prepared Cu-CDs can be used as probes for the ratiometric sensing sensitively determination of Trp. Meanwhile, Fig. 4C shows that a good linear relationship in the concentration range from 0.5 to 250 μM (y = 0.0175x + 1.0311, r = 0.997, n = 3). The limit of detection (LOD) of 275 nM was obtained based on a 3δ/S (δ is the standard deviation of the blank signal and S is the slope of the linear calibration plot), which was comparable or better than most of existing fluorescence probes for Trp.

To assess the selectivity of Cu-CDs toward different substances, such as Trp, Leu, Glu, Cys, Cys-Cys, Asp, Tyr, AA, and glucose were studied under the same procedure to that of Trp detection mentioned above. The fluorescence and SOS intensity responses were recorded and converted to the ratio of Fluorescence/SOS. As shown in Fig. 4D, as the potential interfering substances, the ratio of Fluorescence/SOS of addition each of these substances have similar to the blank Cu-CDs solutions except Trp. So addition these substances to Cu-CDs solutions have no obvious fluorescence enhancement. Different from other substances, the addition of Trp brings significant fluorescence enhancement. Fig. 4D also shows that the ratio of Fluorescence/SOS of addition a mixture of all substances, fluorescence enhancing ability is equal to the addition of Trp alone. These results indicated that the ratiometric probe has high sensitivity and selectivity for Trp detection by fluorescence enhancement effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible fluorescence enhancement mechanism for Trp
ce:section.ce:para: Carboxyl and amino groups are known to exist in Trp and there are also π-electron conjugate systems in this one. However, other substances such as amino acids to be detected do not contain or contain a small π-electron conjugated system. The XPS spectra illustrated the presence of Cu2+ in Cu-CDs. When Trp and Cu- CDs consist in a same system, chelation occur in the carboxyl and amino groups of Trp and Cu2+ in CDs, and bring them together to form a stable structure. In this structure, the fluorophore in Cu-CDs increases luminescence with the participation of π-electron conjugated system. The π-electron conjugate system in Trp will act to enhance the fluorescence intensity of Cu-CDs. Fig. 4E shows the variation of absorbance value with before and after addition of Trp in Cu-CDs. The UV–vis spectrum displayed the absorbance increased explained the fluorescence intensity enhancement with the addition of Trp in Cu-CDs. Fig. 4F shows that there is no large change in the average fluorescence lifetime of the Cu-CDs in the absence or presence of Trp, indicating a static sensing mechanism. The static mechanism occurs when Cu-CDs interacts with Trp to form a complex. Moreover, a slight increase in the average fluorescence lifetime of the Cu-CDs also indicates the occurrence of enhancement.

The as-synthesized Cu-CDs was not merely determination for Trp but also have an amazing effect in Fe(III). The SOS intensity with addition of Fe(III) into the present Cu-CDs aqueous solution can be also enhanced in different peak. The response rate was first investigated, the experimental procedure was the same as that of Trp, except that the amount of Fe(III) added was 200 μL. As it can be seen in Fig. 5A，the ratios of SOS/fluorescence almost have no changed after 10 min. So 10 min was determined as the best suitable reaction time. Under optimized conditions, Fig. 5B shows the fluorescence and SOS spectra with the same excitation of 308 nm, with increasing concentration of Fe(III) added, the SOS intensity of Cu-CDs at 617 nm could be enhance, while the peak of fluorescence at 396 nm was kept constant. Interestingly, this is the exact opposite of Trp detection. So another ratiometric sensing application was establish in the same probe. The ratio of SOS/fluorescence can be used to expression the change of SOS intensity with addition for different concentration of Fe(III). As shown in Fig. 5C, a good linearity of SOS enhancement between the ratio of SOS/fluorescence and Fe(III) concentration was obtained when the ranged from 0.1 to 50 μM (y = 0.012x + 1.0327, r = 0.9937, n = 3), and the limit of detection was estimated to be 46 nM.

To further assess the selectivity of the new ratiometric probe, some metal ions including Fe3+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, K+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Na+, Pb2+, Fe2+, and Zn2+ have been mixed separately with Cu-CDs to detection of fluorescence and SOS intensity. Fig. 5D shows the ratio of SOS/fluorescence with different metal ions as the same concentration. Those results strongly indicates that only Fe(III) could enhance the SOS intensity at 617 nm to make the ratio of SOS/fluorescence higher than blank, while the ratio of SOS/fluorescence of other metal ions was the same with blank. Selective detection of Fe(III) by SOS enhancement mechanisms was achieved. Fig. 5D also shows that the ratio of SOS/fluorescence of addition a mixture of all metal ions, SOS enhancing ability is resemble to the addition of Fe(III) alone. These experiments illustrated the ratiometric sensing probe was more sensitive and dependable for the detection of Fe(III).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible fluorescence enhancement mechanism for Fe(III)
ce:section.ce:para: The SOS enhancement mechanism on addition of Fe(III) possible attributed to PET (Photo-induced electron transfer) “OFF” phenomenon [45]. PET is an excited state electron transfer process by which an excited electron is transferred from donor to acceptor. When a photon excites an electron orbital, an electron in a ground state orbital can be excited to a higher energy orbital. This excited state leaves a vacancy in a ground state orbital that can be filled by an electron donor. It produces an electron in a high energy orbital which can be donated to an electron acceptor. In this work, electrons excites from the nitrogen donor atoms to the system of the receptor unit. Fe(III) cannot induce electron or energy transfer of receptor molecules because of inactive photophysically. Therefore, the intervention of Fe(III) can prevent intramolecular charge transfer. As we all know, there are many holes on the surface of carbon dots. Fe(III) was enter into the surface holes of the Cu-CDs and firmed together, thus combined with hydroxyl, carboxyl, and nitrogen donor atoms containing the lone pairs, their electron-donating ability is suppressed. Because of the electron-withdrawing ability of Fe(III), the charge transfer inside the molecule is further suppressed. Therefore, the PET “OFF” phenomenon has appeared. Characterization of Cu-CDs shows the presence of the above functional groups. The occurrence of Fe(III) through the PET “OFF” phenomenon prevents the internal electron transfer from causing the SOS of the receptor molecules to be affected, thereby achieving an enhanced SOS effect. Fig. 5E shows an UV–vis absorption of a system with or without the addition of Fe(III) in Cu-CDs system. When Fe(III) was added to the Cu-CDs system, the absorbance value increased significantly, indicating that the addition of Fe(III) enhanced the SOS intensity.

A standard addition method was applied for the detection of Trp in milk samples and Fe(III) in lake water samples. The analytical results of these samples were shown in Table 1 and Table 2 . The average recoveries of Trp residues in milk 1 and milk 2 were 99.8% ~101.1% and 98.9% ~100.9%, respectively. The average recoveries of Fe(III) residues in lake water 1 and lake water 2 were 99.2% ~100.6% and 99.3% ~100.4%, respectively. Relative standard deviations (RSDs) of detection for these samples were both lower than 2.3%. The data showed that there are no significant differences between added and analyzed values.

In conclusion, a new water-soluble Cu-CDs have been successfully synthesized through a one-step thermolysis method using Na2[Cu(EDTA)] and AA as the reactants. The doping of Cu not only improves the fluorescence quantum yield, but also promotes electron transfer ability and chelation with the surface group of tryptophan. The Cu-CDs was used as a ratiometric sensing probe to detection of Trp and Fe(III) through the separately enhancement of fluorescence and SOS intensity from two separated wavelengths, 396 and 617 nm. This work shows a wide linear range and low detection limit. The ratiometric method provides the advantage of selectivity, sensitivity and repeatability, and has been successfully applied to detect Trp and Fe(III) in real samples with satisfactory results, demonstrating great potential application in real analysis in the future.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the Analysis and Testing Foundation of Kunming.
University of Science and Technology (2018M20172118065), Yunnan Province, China.
